title: Tapping for the Money Fight That Keeps Returning
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the recurring argument about money in a partnership
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the money argument that comes back in the same form, with the same points made, and without resolution. Taps through the exhaustion of incompatible money histories and toward the possibility that different can still be workable.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the money argument with a partner has happened enough times that both people know the script — and nothing has resolved.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the money argument that comes back in the same form, with the same points on each side, and without the resolution that would make the next one unnecessary. Taps through the exhaustion of incompatible money histories and toward the possibility that different doesn't have to mean stuck.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point on the side of your hand and say each statement once:
Even though we keep having the same money argument and I don't know how to reach him from where I am, I accept this is where we are.
Even though our money histories are different in ways that keep creating friction, something more is possible.
Even though I am tired of explaining what feels obvious to me, I am open to a different approach.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: We keep having this same fight.
Side of Eye: He doesn't see money the way I do.
Under Eye: Our histories are too different.
Under Nose: I am tired of explaining this.
Chin: The same argument keeps returning.
Collarbone: I feel unheard about this.
Under Arm: I don't know how we get past this.
Top of Head: We can't agree and I don't know how.
Reframe
Eyebrow: We have different money histories, both of them real.
Side of Eye: Different doesn't mean irreconcilable.
Under Eye: I can hear his history without agreeing.
Under Nose: There is common ground, even if it takes finding.
Chin: This conversation can land differently.
Collarbone: We both want security — the routes look different.
Under Arm: I can hold my view and still be curious about his.
Top of Head: Different money histories can share a life.
Write one thing about money you don't know the answer to — something worth hearing his perspective on.
Where this comes from
Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).
